competence_markers.description: The student makes their bed daily without being reminded
The student's room is maintained at an acceptable standard — floor clear, belongings in their places, bed made
The student performs at least one regular household cleaning task on schedule without reminders
The student practices personal hygiene independently and consistently — handwashing, dental care, bathing, clean clothes
The student picks up after themselves in common areas of the house without being told
The student demonstrates the 10-minute reset habit — returning items to their places before bed
The student handles visible messes (crumbs, spills, full trash) without being asked

cross_connections.description: Every skill in this packet is a habit. Building and maintaining habits is the core IC1.1 skill applied in a domestic context.
Maintaining cleanliness daily, whether you feel like it or not, is discipline in its simplest and most honest form.
Kitchen cleanup is both a cooking skill and a cleaning skill. The student who cleans the kitchen after preparing food is practicing 2.3.1 and 2.3.2 simultaneously.
A clean space reveals maintenance problems early — a dripping faucet or a damaged wall is visible in a clean room and invisible in a messy one.
Cleaning when nobody is watching is integrity at its most basic. You do not clean because someone is checking. You clean because it is the right condition for your space.

content.why_this_matters:
Your room is yours. Your desk is yours. Your belongings are yours. And keeping them clean and organized is your job — not your parent's job, not a chore that is done for you. It is yours.

Clean is the default. That means a clean room is normal, not special. A messy room is not "how kids are" — it is something that needs to be fixed. The goal is not perfection. The goal is that your space works: you can find what you need, the floor is clear, the bed is made, and nothing is growing mold in a forgotten corner.

You are also going to learn to help clean the rest of the house — wiping surfaces, sweeping floors, taking out trash. These are not punishments. They are the normal work of living in a home with other people. Everyone contributes. Everyone cleans up after themselves. Everyone does their part.

This sounds simple. It is. And it is the foundation of every other kind of discipline you will ever build.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Personal space ownership** — the student maintains their own space (room, desk, belongings) to an acceptable standard
2. **Making the bed** — daily, first thing, as the smallest act of order
3. **Basic cleaning participation** — wiping, sweeping, taking out trash as normal household contributions
4. **Personal hygiene habits** — handwashing, dental care, bathing, clean clothes as personal responsibilities
5. **Organization fundamentals** — everything has a place; the student returns things to their places
6. **The default-clean mindset** — clean is normal, mess is a problem to solve, not a state to accept
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Making Your Bed

Every morning, first thing, make your bed. Not after breakfast. Not when you feel like it. First thing.

This is the smallest act of order. It takes less than two minutes. When it is done, your room already looks better. You have already accomplished something before the day has even started. And when you come back to your room at the end of the day, it looks like a place someone cares about — because someone does. You.

**How to make a bed:**
1. Pull the sheets up and smooth them flat
2. Pull up the blanket or comforter and smooth it
3. Put the pillow at the top, neatly
4. Done

It does not need to be military-perfect. It needs to be done. Every single day.

### Part 2: Everything Has a Place

The secret to a clean room is not cleaning. It is organization. When everything has a specific place it belongs, keeping the room clean is simple: you use something, you put it back where it goes.

**Setting up the system:**
- Books go on the bookshelf or in a specific bin
- Toys go in their specific containers or on their specific shelves
- Clothes go in the dresser, closet, or hamper — never on the floor
- School supplies go in their designated spot
- Art supplies have their home
- Shoes go by the door or in the closet

Your mentor will help you set up this system. After that, maintaining it is your responsibility. If you take something out, you put it back. This is not a rule that needs enforcing — it is a habit that becomes automatic.

**The 10-minute reset:** Before bed each night, spend 10 minutes putting everything back where it belongs. This prevents the gradual slide from "mostly clean" to "completely trashed" that happens when small messes accumulate. Ten minutes a day keeps you from needing an hour on the weekend.

### Part 3: Basic Cleaning Skills

You live in a house with other people. The house needs to be clean. You contribute to that — not because you are being punished, but because that is what people who live together do.

**Skills you will learn and practice regularly:**

- **Wiping surfaces.** Spray and wipe countertops, tables, desks. Use a clean cloth or paper towel. Wipe in one direction, not circles (circles just push the dirt around).
- **Sweeping.** Start from the edges of the room and sweep toward the center, then sweep the pile into a dustpan. Short, firm strokes work better than big dramatic sweeps.
- **Taking out trash.** When the trash is full, take it out. When you see the trash is getting full, take it out before it overflows. This is observation in action.
- **Picking up.** Anything on the floor that should not be on the floor gets picked up. This applies to common areas of the house, not just your room.
- **Loading and unloading the dishwasher.** Or washing dishes by hand — whatever your household uses. You learn where dishes go, and you put them there.

### Part 4: Personal Hygiene

Your body needs care, and that care is your responsibility. These are not suggestions — they are standards.

- **Wash your hands** with soap after using the bathroom, before eating, and after being outside. Every time. This is the single most effective way to prevent illness.
- **Brush your teeth** twice a day — morning and night. For two full minutes each time. Dental health problems that start in childhood compound for a lifetime.
- **Bathe or shower** regularly. Daily is standard; your mentor will set the expectation for your age.
- **Wear clean clothes.** Dirty clothes go in the hamper. You do not wear the same shirt three days in a row because it is on the floor and convenient.
- **Cover your mouth** when you cough or sneeze — into your elbow, not your hand.

These habits protect you and the people around you. They are non-negotiable.

### Part 5: The Clean-Is-Default Mindset

Here is the idea that holds everything in this packet together: **clean is not something you achieve. It is something you maintain.**

A messy room is not a disaster to be cleaned up occasionally. It is a sign that the daily maintenance has slipped. The fix is not a massive cleaning session — it is restoring the daily habits: make the bed, put things away, do the 10-minute reset.

When you see something that needs cleaning — crumbs on the counter, a spill on the floor, a full trash can — you handle it. You do not wait for someone to tell you. You do not walk past it. You see it, and you take care of it. This is called taking responsibility for your environment, and it is one of the most important habits you will ever build.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Bed-Making
**Developing well:** The bed is made every morning before the child leaves the room. No reminders needed. It has become automatic.

**Developing slowly:** The child makes the bed when reminded but forgets otherwise, even after weeks of practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the expectation clear and non-negotiable? If the child sometimes gets away with not making the bed, it will never become automatic. Consistency of enforcement is the key variable.

### 2. Room Maintenance
**Developing well:** The room is consistently clean at bedtime. The 10-minute reset is happening. Belongings return to their places.

**Developing slowly:** The room cycles between clean (after a major cleanup) and messy (the rest of the time).

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the organizational system working? Does every item have a clear home? Is the system simple enough for the child to maintain independently? If a five-year-old cannot figure out where something goes in under five seconds, the system is too complex.

### 3. Household Cleaning Participation
**Developing well:** The child performs their assigned household task on schedule without reminders. They see messes in common areas and handle them without being told.

**Developing slowly:** Every task requires a prompt. The child does not notice messes in common spaces.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you assigned a specific, consistent task (not rotating random tasks)? Consistency builds habit. Variation requires active memory, which is a higher cognitive load than automatic habit.

### 4. Personal Hygiene
**Developing well:** Handwashing, tooth-brushing, and bathing happen on schedule without reminders. The child wears clean clothes daily.

**Developing slowly:** Every hygiene task requires a prompt even after weeks of practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the routines anchored to specific triggers? "Brush teeth after breakfast" is easier to automate than "brush teeth in the morning." Attach hygiene tasks to existing routine events for faster habit formation.
